title: SQL Syntax
description: Represents core query syntax for Syntax, giving the theming engine a named SQL construct.

title: SQL Select
description: Represents the SQL construct `SELECT`, which chooses columns or expressions for a result set.

title: SQL Select Distinct
real_syntax: SELECT DISTINCT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name;
code_examples:
SELECT Count(*) AS DistinctCountries
FROM (SELECT DISTINCT Country FROM Customers);

SELECT DISTINCT column1, column2, ...
FROM table_name;
description: Represents the SQL construct `SELECT DISTINCT`, which returns only unique result rows for selected expressions.

title: SQL Where
real_syntax: SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name WHERE condition;
description: Represents the SQL construct `WHERE`, which filters rows before grouping or projection.

title: SQL Order By
real_syntax: SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name ORDER BY column1, column2, ... ASC|DESC;
code_examples:
SELECT * FROM Products
ORDER BY Price;

SELECT * FROM Products
ORDER BY Price DESC;

SELECT * FROM Products
ORDER BY ProductName;

SELECT * FROM Products
ORDER BY ProductName DESC;

SELECT * FROM Customers
ORDER BY Country, CustomerName;

SELECT * FROM Customers
ORDER BY Country ASC, CustomerName DESC;
description: Represents the SQL construct `ORDER BY`, which sorts query results by one or more expressions.

title: SQL And
real_syntax: SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name WHERE condition1 AND condition2 AND condition3 ...;
code_examples:
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Brazil'
AND City = 'Rio de Janeiro'
AND CustomerID > 50;

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Spain' AND (CustomerName LIKE 'G%' OR CustomerName LIKE 'R%');

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Spain' AND CustomerName LIKE 'G%' OR CustomerName LIKE 'R%';
description: Represents the SQL construct `AND`, which requires both filter conditions to be true.

title: SQL Or
real_syntax: SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name WHERE condition1 OR condition2 OR condition3 ...;
code_examples:
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE City = 'Berlin' OR CustomerName LIKE 'G%' OR Country = 'Norway';

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Spain' AND (CustomerName LIKE 'G%' OR CustomerName LIKE 'R%');

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE Country = 'Spain' AND CustomerName LIKE 'G%' OR CustomerName LIKE 'R%';
description: Represents the SQL construct `OR`, which accepts rows where at least one condition is true.

title: SQL Not
real_syntax: SELECT column1, column2, ... FROM table_name WHERE NOT condition;
code_examples:
SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE NOT Country = 'Spain';

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerName NOT LIKE 'A%';

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE CustomerID NOT BETWEEN 10 AND 60;

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE City NOT IN ('Paris', 'London');

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE NOT CustomerID > 50;

SELECT * FROM Customers
WHERE NOT CustomerId < 50;
description: Represents the SQL construct `NOT`, which negates a filter condition.

title: SQL Insert Into

code_examples:

INSERT INTO table_name (column1, column2, column3, ...)
VALUES (value1, value2, value3, ...);

INSERT INTO table_name
VALUES (value1, value2, value3, ...);
description: Represents the SQL construct `INSERT INTO`, which adds new rows to a table.

title: SQL Joins
description: Represents multi-table query composition for Joins, giving the theming engine a named SQL construct.

title: SQL Inner Join
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 INNER JOIN table2 ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;
code_examples:
SELECT Orders.OrderID, Customers.CustomerName, Shippers.ShipperName
FROM Orders
INNER JOIN Customers ON Orders.CustomerID = Customers.CustomerID
INNER JOIN Shippers ON Orders.ShipperID = Shippers.ShipperID;

SELECT column_name(s)
FROM table1
INNER JOIN table2
ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;
description: Represents the SQL construct `INNER JOIN`, which keeps only rows that match on both joined sides.

title: SQL Left Join
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 LEFT JOIN table2 ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;
code_examples:
SELECT column_name(s)
FROM table1
LEFT JOIN table2
ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;

SELECT Customers.CustomerName, Orders.OrderID
FROM Customers
LEFT JOIN Orders ON Customers.CustomerID = Orders.CustomerID
ORDER BY Customers.CustomerName;

SELECT Customers.CustomerName, Orders.OrderID
FROM Customers
LEFT JOIN Orders
ON Customers.CustomerID = Orders.CustomerID
WHERE Orders.CustomerID IS NULL;
description: Represents the SQL construct `LEFT JOIN`, which keeps all left-side rows and matching right-side data.

title: SQL Right Join
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 RIGHT JOIN table2 ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;
code_examples:
SELECT column_name(s)
FROM table1
RIGHT JOIN table2
ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name;

SELECT Orders.OrderID, Employees.LastName, Employees.FirstName
FROM Orders
RIGHT JOIN Employees ON Orders.EmployeeID = Employees.EmployeeID
ORDER BY Orders.OrderID;
description: Represents the SQL construct `RIGHT JOIN`, which keeps all right-side rows and matching left-side data.

title: SQL Full Join
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 FULL JOIN table2 ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name WHERE condition;
code_examples:
SELECT column_name(s)
FROM table1
FULL JOIN table2
ON table1.column_name = table2.column_name
WHERE condition;

SELECT Customers.CustomerName, Orders.OrderID
FROM Customers
FULL JOIN Orders
ON Customers.CustomerID = Orders.CustomerID;
description: Represents multi-table query composition for Full Join, giving the theming engine a named SQL construct.

title: SQL Self Join
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 T1, table1 T2 WHERE condition;
code_examples:
SELECT A.CustomerName AS CustomerName1, B.CustomerName AS CustomerName2, A.City
FROM Customers A, Customers B
WHERE A.CustomerID <> B.CustomerID
AND A.City = B.City
ORDER BY A.City;
description: Represents multi-table query composition for Self Join, giving the theming engine a named SQL construct.

title: SQL Union
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 UNION SELECT column_name(s) FROM table2;
code_examples:
SELECT Country FROM Customers
UNION
SELECT Country FROM Suppliers
ORDER BY Country;

SELECT City, Country FROM Customers
WHERE Country='Germany'
UNION
SELECT City, Country FROM Suppliers
WHERE Country='Germany'
ORDER BY City;

SELECT 'Customer' AS Type, ContactName, City, Country
FROM Customers
UNION
SELECT 'Supplier', ContactName, City, Country
FROM Suppliers;
description: Represents the SQL construct `UNION`, which combines result sets while removing duplicates.

title: SQL Union All
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table1 UNION ALL SELECT column_name(s) FROM table2;
code_examples:
SELECT Country FROM Customers
UNION ALL
SELECT Country FROM Suppliers
ORDER BY Country;

SELECT City, Country FROM Customers
WHERE Country='Germany'
UNION ALL
SELECT City, Country FROM Suppliers
WHERE Country='Germany'
ORDER BY City;
description: Represents the SQL construct `UNION ALL`, which combines result sets without removing duplicates.

title: SQL Group By
real_syntax: SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2), column3, ... FROM table_name WHERE condition GROUP BY column1, column3 ORDER BY column_name;
code_examples:
SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2), column3, ...
FROM table_name
WHERE condition
GROUP BY column1, column3
ORDER BY column_name;

SELECT Country, COUNT(CustomerID) AS [Number of Customers]
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Country
ORDER BY COUNT(CustomerID) DESC;

SELECT Shippers.ShipperName, COUNT(Orders.OrderID) AS NumberOfOrders
FROM Orders
LEFT JOIN Shippers
ON Orders.ShipperID = Shippers.ShipperID
GROUP BY ShipperName;
description: Represents the SQL construct `GROUP BY`, which groups rows so aggregate calculations can run per group.

title: SQL Having
real_syntax: SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2), column3, ... FROM table_name WHERE condition GROUP BY column1, column3 HAVING condition -- The condition on grouped data ORDER BY column_name;
code_examples:
SELECT column1, aggregate_function(column2), column3, ...
FROM table_name
WHERE condition
GROUP BY column1, column3
HAVING condition -- The condition on grouped data
ORDER BY column_name;

SELECT Country, COUNT(CustomerID) AS [Number of Customers]
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Country
HAVING COUNT(CustomerID) > 5;

SELECT Country, COUNT(CustomerID) AS [Number of Customers]
FROM Customers
GROUP BY Country
HAVING COUNT(CustomerID) > 5
ORDER BY COUNT(CustomerID) DESC;

SELECT Employees.LastName, COUNT(Orders.OrderID) AS NumberOfOrders
FROM Orders
INNER JOIN Employees ON Orders.EmployeeID = Employees.EmployeeID
GROUP BY LastName
HAVING COUNT(Orders.OrderID) > 10;

SELECT Employees.LastName, COUNT(Orders.OrderID) AS NumberOfOrders
FROM Orders
INNER JOIN Employees ON Orders.EmployeeID = Employees.EmployeeID
WHERE LastName = 'Davolio' OR LastName = 'Fuller'
GROUP BY LastName
HAVING COUNT(Orders.OrderID) > 25;
description: Represents the SQL construct `HAVING`, which filters grouped rows after aggregation.

title: SQL Exists
real_syntax: SELECT column_name(s) FROM table_name WHERE EXISTS (subquery);

description: Represents the SQL construct `EXISTS`, which checks whether a subquery returns any row.

title: SQL Any

title: SQL All

title: SQL Case
real_syntax: CASE WHEN condition1 THEN result1 WHEN condition2 THEN result2 WHEN conditionN THEN resultN ELSE default_result END;
code_examples:
CASE
  WHEN condition1 THEN result1
  WHEN condition2 THEN result2
  WHEN conditionN THEN resultN
  ELSE default_result
END;

SELECT ProductName, Price,
CASE
  WHEN Price < 20 THEN 'Low Cost'
  WHEN Price BETWEEN 20 AND 50 THEN 'Medium Cost'
  ELSE 'High Cost'
END AS PriceCategory
FROM Products;
description: Represents the SQL construct `CASE`, which chooses a value from conditional branches.

title: CASE
real_syntax: CASE WHEN condition1 THEN result1 WHEN condition2 THEN result2 WHEN conditionN THEN resultN ELSE result END;
code_examples:
SELECT OrderID, Quantity,
CASE
    WHEN Quantity > 30 THEN "The quantity is greater than 30"
    WHEN Quantity = 30 THEN "The quantity is 30"
    ELSE "The quantity is under 30"
END
FROM OrderDetails;

CASE
    WHEN condition1 THEN result1
    WHEN condition2 THEN result2
    WHEN conditionN THEN resultN
    ELSE result
END;

SELECT CustomerName, City, Country
FROM Customers
ORDER BY
(CASE
    WHEN City IS NULL THEN Country
    ELSE City
END);
